Time Series - Individual Project

The Evolution of S&P 500 Dividend Levels: 1871 to Present

Vitor Reis

March 2026

Section 1: Overall Idea

Dividends are cash payments that companies distribute to shareholders from their profits. For most of the twentieth century, dividends were the primary reason to own equities: investors expected stocks to pay a regular income, much like a bond. Today, dividend yields sit near historic lows, and many investors focus purely on capital gains.

This report investigates how S&P 500 dividend levels have evolved over 150 years, asking three central questions:

  • How have nominal and real (inflation-adjusted) dividends grown over time?
  • Has the dividend yield — the income return to investors — changed structurally over history?
  • What do standard time series tools tell us about trend, seasonality and forecastability of dividends?

The analysis covers monthly data from January 1871 to June 2023 (the latest point in the Shiller dataset), passing through the Second Industrial Revolution, two World Wars, the Great Depression, the dot-com bubble, the 2008 Global Financial Crisis and the Covid Pandemic.

Section 2: Sources

# Dataset Source
1 S&P 500 Historical Data (Price, Dividend, Earnings, CPI) Robert Shiller, Yale University
2 Shiller dataset download http://www.econ.yale.edu/~shiller/data/ie_data.xls

Rationale for selecting this dataset: Many sources were considered during the data exploration phase, including FRED (Federal Reserve Economic Data), Bloomberg, and S&P Dow Jones Indices. However, most of these either don’t have data before the 1920s, require paid subscriptions, or do not include CPI data for inflation adjustments. The Shiller S&P 500 dataset was selected because it is considered standard for long-run US equity research. It has been maintained continuously since the 1980s, includes inflation data (CPI) enabling real-term comparisons, provides monthly granularity back to 1871, and is freely available.

R Libraries used: readxl, dplyr, ggplot2, lubridate, scales, forecast, tseries

Section 3: Data Acquisition and Processing

Downloading the data

# Packages needed
needed = c("readxl", "dplyr", "ggplot2", "lubridate", "scales", "forecast", "tseries")

# Only install in case is missing
for (p in needed) 
  {if (!requireNamespace(p, quietly = TRUE)) install.packages(p, repos = "https://cran.r-project.org")}

# Load Libraries 
library(readxl)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(lubridate)
library(scales)
library(forecast)
library(tseries)

# Download Shiller data 
shiller_file = "ie_data.xls"
download.file(
  url      = "http://www.econ.yale.edu/~shiller/data/ie_data.xls",
  destfile = shiller_file,
  mode     = "wb")

Cleaning and preparing the data

The Shiller Excel file uses a YYYY.MM format for dates (e.g. 1871.01 = January 1871). The dividend column reports annualised dividends per share in nominal USD.

# Read: data begins on row 8; first 7 rows are headers/notes
raw_shiller = read_excel(shiller_file, sheet = "Data", skip = 7, col_names = TRUE)

# Keep only the first 6 columns and rename
raw_shiller = raw_shiller[, 1:6]
colnames(raw_shiller) = c("date_raw", "price", "dividend", "earnings", "cpi", "long_rate")

# Analyse dates and convert columns to numeric
df = raw_shiller %>%
  filter(!is.na(date_raw)) %>%
  mutate(
    date_num = suppressWarnings(as.numeric(date_raw)),
    year     = floor(date_num),
    month    = pmax(1L, pmin(12L, round((date_num - floor(date_num)) * 100))),
    date     = make_date(year, month, 1L),
    price    = as.numeric(price),
    dividend = as.numeric(dividend),
    earnings = as.numeric(earnings),
    cpi      = as.numeric(cpi)
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(date), !is.na(dividend), dividend > 0, year >= 1871) %>%
  arrange(date)

# Inflation-adjust all figures to the most recent month's CPI
base_cpi = tail(df$cpi, 1)

df = df %>%
  mutate(
    real_dividend = dividend * (base_cpi / cpi),
    real_price    = price    * (base_cpi / cpi),
    div_yield     = dividend / price * 100,        # percent
    payout_ratio  = ifelse(earnings > 0, dividend / earnings, NA_real_)
  )

cat("Monthly observations:", nrow(df), "\n")
## Monthly observations: 1830
cat("Period:", format(min(df$date), "%b %Y"), "to", format(max(df$date), "%b %Y"), "\n")
## Period: Jan 1871 to Jun 2023

Data preview

df %>%
  select(date, price, dividend, real_dividend, div_yield) %>%
  tail(12) %>%
  mutate(
    price         = round(price, 1),
    dividend      = round(dividend, 2),
    real_dividend = round(real_dividend, 2),
    div_yield     = round(div_yield, 2)
  )
## # A tibble: 12 × 5
##    date       price dividend real_dividend div_yield
##    <date>     <dbl>    <dbl>         <dbl>     <dbl>
##  1 2022-07-01 3912.     64.4          66.4      1.65
##  2 2022-08-01 4159.     64.9          66.8      1.56
##  3 2022-09-01 3850.     65.3          67.2      1.7 
##  4 2022-10-01 3726.     65.8          67.4      1.77
##  5 2022-11-01 3918.     66.4          68.0      1.69
##  6 2022-12-01 3912.     66.9          68.8      1.71
##  7 2023-01-01 3961.     67.4          68.7      1.7 
##  8 2023-02-01 4080.     67.8          68.7      1.66
##  9 2023-03-01 3969.     68.2          69.0      1.72
## 10 2023-04-01 4122.     68.4          68.8      1.66
## 11 2023-05-01 4146.     68.5          68.8      1.65
## 12 2023-06-01 4345.     68.7          68.7      1.58

Annual averages

For most of the analysis we use annual averages to smooth out within-year noise. We also calculate growth rates and log-dividends, which are needed later for the trend model and ARIMA.

annual = df %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(
    dividend      = mean(dividend,      na.rm = TRUE), 
    real_dividend = mean(real_dividend, na.rm = TRUE),
    price         = mean(price,         na.rm = TRUE),
    div_yield     = mean(div_yield,     na.rm = TRUE),
    payout_ratio  = mean(payout_ratio,  na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate( 
    div_growth      = (dividend      / lag(dividend)      - 1) * 100, # Nominal Dividend year-over-year percentage change 
    real_div_growth = (real_dividend / lag(real_dividend) - 1) * 100, # Real Dividend year-over-year percentage change
    log_div         = log(dividend)
  )

Section 4: Exploratory Data Analysis

Nominal dividend levels

The chart below shows the annualised S&P 500 dividend per share in nominal USD. Key historical crises are highlighted.

# Shaded crisis periods
crises = data.frame(
  xmin = c(1929, 2007, 2020),
  xmax = c(1933, 2009, 2021),
  label = c("Great Depression", "GFC", "COVID"))

ggplot(annual, aes(x = year, y = dividend)) +
  geom_rect(data = crises,
            aes(xmin = xmin, xmax = xmax, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf),
            inherit.aes = FALSE,
            fill = "tomato", alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_line(colour = "#2C7BB6", linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_text(data = crises,
            aes(x = (xmin + xmax) / 2, y = max(annual$dividend) * 0.85,
                label = label),
            size = 2.8, colour = "tomato4") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1880, 2030, 20)) +
  labs(
    title    = "S&P 500 Dividends Per Share (Nominal USD)",
    subtitle = "Annual averages, 1871 to present",
    x = NULL, y = "Dividends per share (USD)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

Three observations stand out immediately:

  • The Great Depression (1929-1933) caused the single largest dividend collapse in the dataset, with payouts falling more than 55%.
  • The long post-WWII expansion drove a steady nominal increase from the 1950s onwards.
  • The 2008 GFC caused the second largest cut, led by the financial sector suspending payouts.

Real (inflation-adjusted) dividends

Adjusting for inflation shows how much buying power dividends have actually delivered over time.

# Shaded crisis bands
crises = data.frame(
  xmin = c(1929, 2007, 2020),
  xmax = c(1933, 2009, 2021),
  label = c("Great Depression", "GFC", "COVID"))

ggplot(annual, aes(x = year, y = real_dividend)) +
  geom_rect(data = crises,
            aes(xmin = xmin, xmax = xmax, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf),
            inherit.aes = FALSE,
            fill = "tomato", alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_line(colour = "#1A9641", linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_area(alpha = 0.1, fill = "#1A9641") +
  geom_text(data = crises,
            aes(x = (xmin + xmax) / 2, y = max(annual$real_dividend) * 0.85,
                label = label),
            size = 2.8, colour = "tomato4") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1880, 2030, 20)) +
  labs(
    title    = paste0("S&P 500 Dividends Per Share (Real, ", format(max(df$date), "%Y"), " USD)"),
    subtitle = "Inflation-adjusted using Shiller CPI",
    x = NULL, y = "Real dividends per share (USD)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

In real terms, the growth is striking. Real dividends were broadly flat from the 1870s through the 1950s, around $10-15 per share. The high-inflation 1970s eroded real payouts — nominal dividends were rising, but not fast enough to beat inflation. The post-war era saw a gradual increase, and the most dramatic acceleration has occurred since the 2010s, with real dividends roughly tripling from ~$20 to over $60.

Dividend yield over time

The dividend yield (dividends / price) measures how much income you get per dollar invested — often more useful than the dividend level alone.

mean_yield = mean(annual$div_yield, na.rm = TRUE)
recent_mean = mean(annual$div_yield[annual$year >= 1995], na.rm = TRUE)

ggplot(annual, aes(x = year, y = div_yield)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#756BB1", linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_area(alpha = 0.12, fill = "#756BB1") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = mean_yield, linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.7) +
  annotate("text", x = 1875, y = mean_yield + 0.25,
           label = paste0("Full-sample mean: ", round(mean_yield, 1), "%"),
           size = 3.2, hjust = 0, colour = "grey30") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = recent_mean, linetype = "dotted",
             colour = "#D01C8B", linewidth = 0.7) +
  annotate("text", x = 1875, y = recent_mean - 0.3,
           label = paste0("Post-1995 mean: ", round(recent_mean, 1), "%"),
           size = 3.2, hjust = 0, colour = "#D01C8B") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1880, 2030, 20)) +
  labs(
    title    = "S&P 500 Dividend Yield",
    subtitle = "Dividends / Price x 100 — a persistent regime shift is visible after 1995",
    x = NULL, y = "Dividend yield (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

The dividend yield chart shows a clear structural shift. From 1871 until the mid-1990s, the yield fluctuated around a mean of roughly 4.3%. Since 1995, it has stayed persistently below 1.8% — less than half the historical average. This reflects the rise of share buybacks, the growth of non-dividend-paying tech companies, and a broad market shift toward valuing growth over income.

Annual dividend growth rates

growth_mean = mean(annual$div_growth, na.rm = TRUE)

annual %>%
  filter(!is.na(div_growth)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = div_growth, fill = div_growth > 0)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = growth_mean, linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "black", linewidth = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("TRUE" = "#4DAC26", "FALSE" = "#D01C8B")) +
  annotate("text", x = 1875, y = growth_mean + 3,
           label = paste0("Mean: ", round(growth_mean, 1), "%"),
           size = 3.2, hjust = 0) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1880, 2030, 20)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Annual Dividend Growth Rate (%)",
    subtitle = "Green = positive growth year, Pink = cut",
    x = NULL, y = "Year-on-year growth (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

On average, nominal dividends have grown at 4.2% per year — well above inflation over the same period (roughly 2.8% annually). However, the distribution is very wide and the worst years (1932, 1938, 2009) saw cuts exceeding 20%.

Share of S&P 500 companies paying dividends

The dividend yield chart shows how much income the index pays — but that hides another fact: fewer companies paying dividends at all. The data below, sourced directly from S&P Dow Jones Indices, tracks the share of S&P 500 constituents paying a dividend each year since 1980.

# Source: S&P Dow Jones Indices - PAYERS sheet
payers = data.frame(
  year    = c(1980, 1985, 1990, 1995, 2000, 2002, 2005, 2010, 2015, 2020, 2025),
  pct_pay = c(93.8, 88.4, 87.6, 86.4, 74.4, 70.2, 77.2, 74.6, 82.7, 76.2, 81.3)
)

ggplot(payers, aes(x = year, y = pct_pay)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#E6550D", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(colour = "#E6550D", size = 2.5) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(55, 100),
                     labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1980, 2025, 5)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Share of S&P 500 Companies Paying Dividends",
    subtitle = "Year-end figures, 1980 to 2025. Source: S&P Dow Jones Indices",
    x = NULL, y = "% of constituents paying a dividend"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

In 1980, 93.8% of S&P 500 companies paid a dividend. That share declined steadily, hitting a low of around 70% in 2001-2002 as the dot-com era brought a wave of non-paying tech companies into the index. Since then it has partially recovered, sitting at 81.3% in 2025. The story is therefore more nuanced than a simple decline: the proportion of payers fell sharply into the early 2000s but has stabilised and even recovered since. This reinforces the dividend yield story — yields are low not just because prices rose, but because a meaningful portion of the index pays nothing at all.

Summary statistics by era

annual %>%
  mutate(Era = cut(year,
                   breaks = c(1870, 1919, 1944, 1969, 1989, 2009, 2019, Inf),
                   labels = c("1871-1919", "1920-1944", "1945-1969",
                              "1970-1989", "1990-2009", "2010-2019",
                              "2020-present (Post-Pandemic)"))) %>%
  group_by(Era) %>%
  summarise(
    `Avg Nominal Div ($)`  = round(mean(dividend, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    `Avg Real Div ($)`     = round(mean(real_dividend, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    `Avg Yield (%)`        = round(mean(div_yield, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    `Avg Growth (%)`       = round(mean(div_growth, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
## # A tibble: 7 × 5
##   Era                   `Avg Nominal Div ($)` `Avg Real Div ($)` `Avg Yield (%)`
##   <fct>                                 <dbl>              <dbl>           <dbl>
## 1 1871-1919                              0.32               10.5            5.18
## 2 1920-1944                              0.64               12.3            5.61
## 3 1945-1969                              1.79               18.8            4.25
## 4 1970-1989                              5.79               22.4            4.1 
## 5 1990-2009                             17.2                30.1            2.12
## 6 2010-2019                             38.8                49.4            1.99
## 7 2020-present (Post-P…                 62.5                67.8            1.62
## # ℹ 1 more variable: `Avg Growth (%)` <dbl>

Section 5: Time Series Analysis

Trend: how fast have dividends grown?

We fit a line through log-dividends to estimate how fast dividends have grown per year on average (the CAGR).

trend_model = lm(log_div ~ year, data = annual) # log div vs year
slope        = coef(trend_model)["year"]    # extract the slope
growth_factor = exp(slope)                  # convert from log-space 
growth_rate  = growth_factor - 1            # decimal growth rate
cagr_nominal = growth_rate * 100            # change to percentage
cat(sprintf("Estimated nominal dividend CAGR (log-linear trend): %.2f%% per year\n", cagr_nominal))
## Estimated nominal dividend CAGR (log-linear trend): 3.88% per year
# Real CAGR
trend_real = lm(log(real_dividend) ~ year, data = annual)
slope_real        = coef(trend_real)["year"]    # extract the slope
growth_factor_real = exp(slope_real)            # convert from log-space
growth_rate_real  = growth_factor_real - 1      # decimal growth rate
cagr_real         = growth_rate_real * 100      # as percentage

cat(sprintf("Estimated real dividend CAGR:                        %.2f%% per year\n", cagr_real))
## Estimated real dividend CAGR:                        1.20% per year
annual %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = dividend)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#2C7BB6") +                          # actual dividend data (blue line)
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x,              # best-fit trend line (black)
              colour = "black", se = TRUE,
              aes(y = exp(log_div))) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = dollar_format()) +                 # log scale on y-axis
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1880, 2030, 20)) +        # tick marks every 20 years
  labs(
    title    = "S&P 500 Dividends — Long-Run Growth Trend",
    subtitle = paste0("Black line = average growth trend (",
                      round(cagr_nominal, 1), "% per year)"),
    x = NULL,
    y = "Dividends per share (log scale, USD)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

Stationarity

Before fitting an ARIMA model, we need to check if the data is stationary (i.e. fluctuates around a stable average). If not, we need to convert it to growth rates first.

log_div_clean = annual$log_div[!is.na(annual$log_div)]        # keep only non-NA log-dividend values
start_year    = min(annual$year[!is.na(annual$log_div)])       # find the earliest year with valid data
ts_log_div    = ts(log_div_clean, start = start_year)          # Turn it into a time series so R treats it as yearly data in order

# Checking if series is stationary
adf_levels = adf.test(ts_log_div) # ADF test on levels
adf_diff   = adf.test(diff(ts_log_div)) # ADF test on differences - meaning year-to-year changes are stationary

cat("ADF test on log-dividend levels:\n")
## ADF test on log-dividend levels:
cat(sprintf("  Statistic: %.4f  |  p-value: %.4f\n", adf_levels$statistic, adf_levels$p.value))
##   Statistic: -2.0561  |  p-value: 0.5530
cat(ifelse(adf_levels$p.value > 0.05,
           "  => Fail to reject unit root. Series is NON-STATIONARY.\n\n",
           "  => Reject unit root. Series is stationary.\n\n"))
##   => Fail to reject unit root. Series is NON-STATIONARY.
cat("ADF test on first-differenced log-dividends (= growth rates):\n")
## ADF test on first-differenced log-dividends (= growth rates):
cat(sprintf("  Statistic: %.4f  |  p-value: %.4f\n", adf_diff$statistic, adf_diff$p.value))
##   Statistic: -6.1716  |  p-value: 0.0100
cat(ifelse(adf_diff$p.value < 0.05,
           "  => Reject unit root. Growth rates ARE stationary. Series is I(1).\n",
           "  => Fail to reject unit root.\n"))
##   => Reject unit root. Growth rates ARE stationary. Series is I(1).

Interpretation: Dividends keep rising over time with no fixed average — the ADF test confirms this (non-stationary). But when we look at the yearly growth rates instead, they do fluctuate around a stable average (stationary). So we need to convert the data into growth rates first — that’s what allows the ARIMA model to work properly.

Seasonal decomposition (STL)

Using monthly data, we split the dividend series into three parts: the long-run trend, a seasonal pattern, and leftover noise.

ts_monthly = ts(
  df$dividend,
  start     = c(year(min(df$date)), month(min(df$date))),
  frequency = 12
)

stl_fit = stl(log(ts_monthly), s.window = "periodic", robust = TRUE)
autoplot(stl_fit) +
  labs(title = "STL Decomposition of Monthly Log-Dividends") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

The decomposition confirms three things:

  1. Trend: dividends have been steadily rising over the full 150-year period.
  2. Seasonal: there’s a small repeating pattern within each year — dividends tend to be slightly higher in Q4.
  3. Remainder: what’s left over after removing trend and seasonality — these spikes line up with major crises like the Great Depression and 2008 GFC.

ARIMA model

We fit an ARIMA model to forecast future dividends. Since dividends trend upward (non-stationary), we first convert them to growth rates (one round of differencing) so the model can work with stable data.

arima_fit = auto.arima(
  ts_log_div,
  d             = 1,
  stepwise      = FALSE,
  approximation = FALSE,
  ic            = "aicc"
)

summary(arima_fit)
## Series: ts_log_div 
## ARIMA(4,1,0) with drift 
## 
## Coefficients:
##          ar1      ar2     ar3      ar4   drift
##       0.8344  -0.6925  0.3637  -0.2996  0.0362
## s.e.  0.0772   0.0988  0.0987   0.0770  0.0074
## 
## sigma^2 = 0.005378:  log likelihood = 183.42
## AIC=-354.84   AICc=-354.26   BIC=-336.7
## 
## Training set error measures:
##                        ME       RMSE        MAE      MPE     MAPE      MASE
## Training set 3.439204e-05 0.07187983 0.05264086 4.179605 10.45397 0.6396275
##                    ACF1
## Training set 0.01766597
checkresiduals(arima_fit)

## 
##  Ljung-Box test
## 
## data:  Residuals from ARIMA(4,1,0) with drift
## Q* = 3.0991, df = 6, p-value = 0.7963
## 
## Model df: 4.   Total lags used: 10
lb = Box.test(residuals(arima_fit), lag = 10, type = "Ljung-Box")
cat(sprintf("Ljung-Box test (lag 10): p-value = %.4f\n", lb$p.value))
## Ljung-Box test (lag 10): p-value = 0.9790
cat(ifelse(lb$p.value > 0.05,
           "Residuals look like white noise. Model is adequate.\n",
           "Some residual autocorrelation remains.\n"))
## Residuals look like white noise. Model is adequate.

Dividend forecast (10 years)

fc = forecast(arima_fit, h = 10)

autoplot(fc) +
  labs(
    title    = "10-Year ARIMA Forecast for Log S&P 500 Dividends",
    subtitle = "Dark band = 80% interval, light band = 95% interval",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Log-Dividend"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

fc_df = as.data.frame(fc)
fc_df$Year           = seq(max(annual$year) + 1, by = 1, length.out = 10)
fc_df$Forecast       = round(exp(fc_df[["Point Forecast"]]), 2)
fc_df$Lower_80       = round(exp(fc_df[["Lo 80"]]), 2)
fc_df$Upper_80       = round(exp(fc_df[["Hi 80"]]), 2)
fc_df$Lower_95       = round(exp(fc_df[["Lo 95"]]), 2)
fc_df$Upper_95       = round(exp(fc_df[["Hi 95"]]), 2)
fc_df[, c("Year","Forecast","Lower_80","Upper_80","Lower_95","Upper_95")]
##      Year Forecast Lower_80 Upper_80 Lower_95 Upper_95
## 2024 2024    68.32    62.19    75.05    59.17    78.88
## 2025 2025    69.82    57.37    84.97    51.71    94.28
## 2026 2026    72.80    56.05    94.56    48.80   108.59
## 2027 2027    75.06    55.44   101.61    47.23   119.29
## 2028 2028    77.54    55.62   108.10    46.65   128.89
## 2029 2029    80.99    57.02   115.04    47.35   138.53
## 2030 2030    84.38    58.62   121.47    48.34   147.31
## 2031 2031    87.45    59.92   127.62    49.05   155.90
## 2032 2032    90.66    61.14   134.44    49.63   165.61
## 2033 2033    94.00    62.30   141.82    50.11   176.32

The prediction intervals get wider over time because long-run forecasts are naturally uncertain. The central forecast simply assumes dividends keep growing at the same historical rate — it’s a reasonable baseline, not an exact prediction.

Forecast vs Actual Comparison

Since the Shiller dataset ends in 2023, we can validate the ARIMA forecast against actual 2024 and 2025 dividend data for the S&P 500.

# Actual S&P 500 data
actuals = data.frame(
  Year       = c(2024, 2025),
  Actual_Div = c(74.83, 79.52)
)

# Extract ARIMA forecast for same years
fc_compare = fc_df[fc_df$Year %in% c(2024, 2025), c("Year", "Forecast", "Lower_95", "Upper_95")]
colnames(fc_compare) = c("Year", "Forecast_Div", "Lo_95", "Hi_95")

# Merge and calculate error
comparison = merge(fc_compare, actuals, by = "Year")
comparison$Error_Pct   = round((comparison$Forecast_Div / comparison$Actual_Div - 1) * 100, 2)
comparison$Within_95CI = comparison$Actual_Div >= comparison$Lo_95 & comparison$Actual_Div <= comparison$Hi_95
comparison
##   Year Forecast_Div Lo_95 Hi_95 Actual_Div Error_Pct Within_95CI
## 1 2024        68.32 59.17 78.88      74.83      -8.7        TRUE
## 2 2025        69.82 51.71 94.28      79.52     -12.2        TRUE
ggplot(comparison, aes(x = Year)) +
  geom_col(aes(y = Actual_Div), fill = "#2C7BB6", alpha = 0.7, width = 0.4) +
  geom_point(aes(y = Forecast_Div), colour = "red", size = 4) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = Lo_95, ymax = Hi_95), width = 0.2, colour = "red") +
  geom_text(aes(y = Actual_Div, label = paste0("$", Actual_Div)),
            vjust = -0.5, colour = "#2C7BB6") +
  geom_text(aes(y = Forecast_Div, label = paste0("$", Forecast_Div)),
            vjust = 1.5, colour = "red") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(2024, 2025)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
  labs(
    title    = "ARIMA Forecast vs Actual Dividends Per Share",
    subtitle = "Blue bars = actual, Red dots = forecast (with 95% CI)",
    x = NULL, y = "Dividends per share (USD)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

Actual S&P 500 dividends per share were $74.83 in 2024 (+6.4% YoY) and $79.52 in 2025 (+6.3% YoY), sourced from S&P Dow Jones Indices. The table above shows the forecast error and whether the actuals fell within the model’s 95% confidence interval, demonstrating how close the ARIMA forecast got.

Hypothesis test: has the dividend yield structurally shifted?

The dividend yield chart in Section 4 showed a clear drop after 1995. Here we use a t-test to check whether the average dividend yield is statistically different before and after 1995.

# Split the yield data into pre-1995 and post-1995
pre_1995  = annual$div_yield[annual$year < 1995]
post_1995 = annual$div_yield[annual$year >= 1995]

# Two-sample t-test: are the means different?
t_result = t.test(pre_1995, post_1995)

cat("Pre-1995 mean yield: ", round(mean(pre_1995, na.rm = TRUE), 2), "%\n")
## Pre-1995 mean yield:  4.82 %
cat("Post-1995 mean yield:", round(mean(post_1995, na.rm = TRUE), 2), "%\n\n")
## Post-1995 mean yield: 1.84 %
cat(sprintf("t-statistic: %.4f\n", t_result$statistic))
## t-statistic: 21.6510
cat(sprintf("p-value:     %.6f\n", t_result$p.value))
## p-value:     0.000000
cat(ifelse(t_result$p.value < 0.05,
           "\n=> Reject the null: the dividend yield mean is significantly different before and after 1995.\n",
           "\n=> Fail to reject the null: no significant difference.\n"))
## 
## => Reject the null: the dividend yield mean is significantly different before and after 1995.
annual %>%
  mutate(regime = ifelse(year < 1995, "Pre-1995", "Post-1995")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = div_yield, fill = regime)) +
  geom_histogram(alpha = 0.6, bins = 30, position = "identity") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mean(pre_1995, na.rm = TRUE),
             linetype = "dashed", colour = "#2C7BB6", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mean(post_1995, na.rm = TRUE),
             linetype = "dashed", colour = "#D01C8B", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Pre-1995" = "#2C7BB6", "Post-1995" = "#D01C8B")) +
  labs(
    title    = "Distribution of Dividend Yields: Pre vs Post 1995",
    subtitle = "Dashed lines = regime means",
    x = "Dividend yield (%)", y = "Count", fill = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

The t-test confirms that the drop in dividend yield after 1995 is real and not just random variation. The post-1995 average is roughly half the historical mean, driven by companies increasingly using buybacks instead of dividends and the rise of tech stocks that don’t pay dividends.

Section 6: Key Findings

  • Long-run nominal growth is about 3.9% per year, but in real terms only around 1.2%. Inflation erodes dividend income over long horizons.

  • The Great Depression was by far the largest dividend shock in 150 years, cutting payouts by more than half. The 2008 GFC was the second worst, driven by bank dividend suspensions.

  • The dividend yield has permanently shifted lower since the mid-1990s, from a historical mean of roughly 4.3% to below 2% today. A t-test confirms this shift is statistically significant. This is not because dividends fell — they kept rising — but because stock prices rose much faster.

  • Dividend levels are non-stationary — they trend upward with no stable average. But growth rates are stationary, so we difference the data once before fitting the ARIMA model.

  • Mild positive autocorrelation exists in annual dividend growth — a good year tends to be followed by another, though the effect is modest.

  • The STL decomposition shows a clear upward trend, a small seasonal pattern (Q4 peak), and crisis-driven spikes in the remainder.

References

  • S&P Dow Jones Indices (2025). U.S. Common Indicated Dividend Payments Q4 2024. https://www.spglobal.com/spdji/

  • GuruFocus (2025). S&P 500 Dividend per Share. https://www.gurufocus.com/economic_indicators/59/sp-500-dividend-per-share

  • S&P Dow Jones Indices. https://www.spglobal.com/spdji/en/documents/additional-material/sp-500-indicated-rate-change.xlsx
